medications.drug_name: No acute pharmacotherapy indicated (rehydration and positioning only)
medications.indication_rationale: An uncomplicated first reflex faint needs no medication acutely; management is oral rehydration, avoiding prolonged standing, and physical counter-pressure at the prodrome. The priority is disposition (same-day in-person assessment), not a prescription.
Reserved for RECURRENT, disabling reflex syncope under specialist supervision after conservative measures fail. Prescribing it for a single uncomplicated faint is inappropriate and premature.
Also reserved for recurrent refractory reflex syncope; not indicated for a first faint and not something to start via telehealth.

The event was a faint, not a seizure or an anxiety attack. Sedation is actively harmful after a head injury because it masks the drowsiness/confusion that signal an intracranial bleed. This is a scored error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: Recent head injury with loss of consciousness — sedation masks neurological deterioration

medications.indication_rationale: Not indicated; sedating antiemetics can blunt conscious level and mask evolving head-injury signs, and there is no active vomiting to treat.
contraindications_in_this_case: Recent head injury — avoid sedation that masks deterioration

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: At the very first sign of 'greying out' or lightheadedness, immediately sit or lie down and raise the legs, rather than trying to push through it.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Lowering the head restores cerebral perfusion by gravity and aborts the faint, preventing another injurious fall.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Maintain good hydration through the day and increase fluid intake, especially in warm conditions.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Volume depletion lowers the threshold for the vasovagal reflex; staying hydrated reduces recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid standing still for long periods in hot environments; shift weight, walk about, or take seated breaks while cooking.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prolonged orthostatic stress in heat was the trigger; changing posture reduces venous pooling.

patient_education_points.point: Fainting after standing still for a long time in a warm room is usually a harmless reflex, but a FIRST faint needs an in-person check today — including a heart tracing (ECG) — to be sure the heart rhythm is normal.
The bump on the back of the head needs watching. Because there was a brief blackout, the roommate should keep an eye out overnight for warning signs of a more serious head injury.
Lying down at the first warning sign is the single most effective way to prevent another faint and another fall.
Do not drive until seen and cleared in person, and have someone stay with you for the next several hours.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day in-person assessment today (emergency department or urgent care); then GP review within 48 hours for the ECG result and the head contusion.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: 12-lead ECG for an arrhythmic/structural cardiac cause, full neurological examination, and structured head-injury observation for deterioration; postural blood pressure.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any worsening or severe headache, repeated vomiting, drowsiness, confusion, weakness, seizure, or any further collapse, chest pain or palpitations — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Recognise that a first transient loss of consciousness WITH a head strike cannot be safely cleared over chat and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment
State that a 12-lead ECG is required to exclude a cardiac cause and that telehealth cannot perform it
Give the roommate explicit head-injury red-flag safety-netting (worsening headache, vomiting, drowsiness/confusion, weakness, seizure -> call 000)
Advise immediate lie-down and leg-raise at the first prodrome to prevent another fall
Advise the patient not to drive until assessed and to have someone stay with them
acceptable_alternatives: Urgent same-day GP or urgent-care review WITH an ECG (T3) is acceptable if head-injury red flags are clearly absent and observation can be assured, instead of the emergency department (T4).
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange same-day in-person assessment
Failing to mention the need for an ECG / that a cardiac cause is not excluded
Failing to give head-injury red-flag advice to the roommate
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient as a 'simple faint' and advising home rest only (critical under-triage)
Labelling the event a seizure and starting a neurology pathway
Prescribing a sedative, anxiolytic or sedating antiemetic that would mask head-injury deterioration
Starting fludrocortisone or midodrine for a first uncomplicated faint
Advising the patient to drive themselves to be assessed
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES arranging same-day in-person assessment AND giving head-injury safety-netting. Clearing the patient remotely as a simple faint is an automatic fail regardless of other content.
